narrative.canopy: Between 900 and 1,100 metres across the Western Ghats — on Wayanad's slopes and higher still where the Nilgiris shoulder begins. At this altitude the monsoon doesn't simply fall; it moves through in layers, soaking the canopy first, then the vine below, then the soil in a sequence that repeats for months.
narrative.land: Forest loam — dark, loose, built up over years by leaf litter that no one cleared. It holds water the way a forest holds water: slowly, deeply, and for longer than cleared ground ever could. The farms where this pepper grows are not farms in the industrial sense — they are managed sections of a forest that is still, largely, a forest.
narrative.hand: The vines climb living trees, not manufactured poles — the older way, before plantations introduced a tidier geometry. Harvest comes once a year, in January, done by hand from trees that have been climbed by the same families across generations. After picking, the berries are laid on traditional mats and sun-dried over several days until they reach the weight and colour and hardness that means they are done.
